frameworks.name: Ham and Garlic Framework
frameworks.definition: Wrap the thing customers need (garlic: continuity) inside the thing they want (ham: a defined short program); the front-end defined offer is a sales vehicle, not the end product.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a tutoring business owner sees customers saying they want ongoing tutoring instead of a short program, that is a demand problem, not an offer problem: the business is failing to sell the defined front-end program, not failing to deliver the continuity product.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Service business running ads to both a short defined program and ongoing continuity
Customers expressing preference for ongoing delivery over defined program
claims.mechanism: Customers who express preference for the back-end product (continuity) are revealing that the front-end defined offer is not compelling enough to attract committed buyers; the real constraint is traffic and conversion, not product design.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that continuity (recurring subscription service) is rarely sold directly from cold advertising: customers virtually never purchase a gym membership for the lifestyle outcome; they buy a 21-day challenge, a 6-week program, or a 28-day fix, and the defined front-end program becomes the sales mechanism that educates them into wanting long-term engagement.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: continuity is rarely sold that way
conditions: Service business selling recurring engagement from cold paid advertising
Customer has not yet experienced the service
claims.mechanism: The defined short program gives the customer a longer period of time inside the product to be educated on the value of long-term engagement; momentum and visible results shift their willingness to commit to ongoing billing.
bounded_by: Warm referral or repeat-customer contexts where trust is pre-established may not require a defined front-end offer

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a tutoring business selling short defined programs (e.g., a 3-week summer program) should bundle the front-end program against a full-year package rather than a single-term package, because the annual framing sets the customer's expectation of the full engagement from the outset.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Service business with natural term structure (e.g., school terms or academic year)
Defined short program already converting on paid ads

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends that a tutoring business offer an outcome guarantee of the form: achieve a target score (e.g., 40 out of 50, or a 4-point improvement) or continue tutoring for free until you do. He asserts this framing is compelling because it transfers performance risk from the buyer to the provider.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: I'll keep tutoring you for free until you get it
conditions: High-stakes outcome service (exam preparation, competitive scoring)
Provider confident in delivery quality and average client retention
Score is measurable and externally verifiable
claims.mechanism: Transferring the performance risk to the provider removes the buyer's primary objection (what if it does not work) and increases perceived probability of achieving the desired outcome, making willingness to pay for a year-long commitment much higher.
bounded_by: Providers with high churn in early weeks will find this guarantee costly
Hormozi acknowledges the fear: what if students keep studying for years without improving; he counters that this is empirically rare

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a defined short-program ad campaign that was previously working starts to decline, the most likely cause is creative fatigue rather than market saturation or offer failure, and the recommended response is to produce more creative variations of the same offer rather than change the offer itself.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: your creative might have just suffered
conditions: Paid ad campaign that had previously generated qualified leads
Same targeting, same audience, declining performance
claims.mechanism: Ad creative fatigues as the target audience has already been exposed to it; variation in hooks and execution refreshes the signal without requiring a new offer.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when Facebook platform targeting constraints prevent filtering by buyer-demographic age (the example: minimum age limit raised so parents of high school students could no longer be isolated from students themselves), the resulting lead pool is structurally wrong regardless of creative quality, causing close rates to collapse to near zero.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: a bunch more students coming through when usually I just want to talk to parents
conditions: B2C service where the buyer (parent) and the end user (student) are different demographics
Paid advertising on Facebook with age-band targeting restrictions imposed by the platform
claims.mechanism: When the ad reaches students instead of parents, the leads cannot convert because students are not the economic decision-maker; the entire sales motion is directed at the wrong avatar, producing zero purchase intent regardless of offer quality.
bounded_by: Does not apply when buyer and end user are the same person

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the fact a large competing tutoring business in the same state uses a one-week or two-week free trial as its front-end offer rather than selling straight into ongoing tutoring confirms that selling defined short programs (ham) to upsell continuity is the dominant market structure, not a workaround.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: they give them the ham, which is what they want, try it, taste it, and then they sell them into long-term thing
conditions: Service category where end users require trial before committing to recurring spend
claims.mechanism: The largest competitors in a market independently converge on the dominant acquisition structure; their survival and scale is indirect evidence that the structure is near-optimal for customer acquisition in that category.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that continuity revenue (subscriptions, ongoing tutoring, memberships) is the underlying goal of most service businesses, but customers rarely self-identify as wanting a subscription; they self-identify as wanting a specific near-term outcome, and this gap between what customers want to buy and what businesses want to sell is the root cause of low conversion on direct continuity offers.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: no person ever bought a gym membership saying, I really want a lifestyle
conditions: Service business attempting to sell recurring subscriptions from cold paid traffic
Customers aware of the general outcome category but not yet committed to ongoing delivery
claims.mechanism: Customers purchase outcomes, not delivery formats; a subscription framed as a subscription triggers resistance because the commitment is indefinite and the benefit is abstract, while a defined program with a clear deliverable triggers purchase because the value is concrete and the risk is bounded.

examples.text: Alex Hormozi tells the ham-and-garlic story to illustrate the ham-and-garlic framework: a boy begs his father for a dog, the father agrees on condition the boy takes full responsibility. The dog gets ticks while the father is away. The grandmother tells the boy to feed the dog garlic to sweat out the ticks. The dog refuses to eat raw garlic. The grandmother tells him to wrap the garlic in ham. The dog eats it, the ticks clear, the day is saved. The moral: give people what they want (ham) in order to give them what they need (garlic). Applied to the tutoring business: the short 3-week program is the ham; the year-long continuity engagement is the garlic.

Alex Hormozi gives the gym industry as a parallel example: no customer has ever walked into a gym and said they want a lifestyle. They buy a 6-week challenge, a 21-day detox, or a 28-day fix. After enrolling, the gym has a window to educate the customer on what really matters. The gym then upgrades them from the short program to a long-term membership once momentum is established (e.g., after losing 20 of 50 desired pounds, the customer is invested and ready to commit to a full solution).

The tutoring business owner reports that after changing Facebook ad targeting from the 35+ age bracket (reaching parents) to a minimum of 25 (the new platform limit), student traffic flooded in instead of parent traffic. Despite the cost per lead dropping by 75%, none of the leads converted because students are not the economic decision-maker for tutoring purchases; parents are. This illustrates that lower cost-per-lead is meaningless when the lead pool is the wrong avatar.

practices.text: If a defined short-program ad campaign was previously working, return to it rather than replacing it with a direct ongoing-service offer when conversion drops.

When building a tutoring offer, state the specific measurable outcome (e.g., a 4-point score improvement or top-10% state ranking) and attach a free-continuation guarantee until the outcome is achieved.
Bundle a short defined front-end program against a full-year package (not a single term) to set the expectation of the full engagement from the first sale.

When Facebook platform targeting restrictions prevent isolating the economically responsible buyer (e.g., parents vs. students), prioritize fixing the audience definition before optimizing creative or offer.
